title: SARIMA: Adding the Seasonality
concept: SARIMA adds seasonal parameters to ARIMA, letting it model patterns that repeat over fixed intervals, like yearly peaks or weekly cycles.

exercises.prompt: Recap: In ARIMA(p, d, q), what does the parameter 'q' control?

options.text: The number of past forecast errors (MA lags) used to adjust predictions
The number of times the data is differenced to remove trends
The number of past values (AR lags) the model looks at

exercises.explain: Spot on! 'q' is the number of past error terms. But what if our data repeats in regular patterns every year? Let's find out!

cards.title: Summer Ice Cream Peaks
cards.body: Suppose you sell ice cream. Every single July, your sales skyrocket. Every December, they drop. An ARIMA model might only look back at the last few days (like June's sales) and miss the big picture. To predict this July's sales, the most important clue is actually *last* July's sales!

cards.title: What is SARIMA?
cards.body: SARIMA stands for Seasonal AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average. It extends ARIMA by adding a second set of parameters to specifically track patterns that repeat over a fixed seasonal cycle (like 12 months for yearly data or 7 days for weekly data).

cards.title: Two Calendars
cards.body: Think of SARIMA as having two calendars. The first calendar looks at day-to-day changes (e.g., comparing today to yesterday). The second calendar looks at cycle-to-cycle changes (e.g., comparing this Friday to last Friday). By tracking both, the model handles both short-term shifts and repeating cycles.

cards.title: Toy Sales Seasonality
cards.body: A toy store sales model uses SARIMA with a seasonality of 12 (for 12 months). When forecasting December sales, the model doesn't just look at November (short-term); it looks back exactly 12 months to last December, capturing the holiday shopping rush.

cards.title: Seasonality is Not Just Weather
cards.body: You might think 'seasonality' only refers to weather seasons like Summer or Winter. In data science, seasonality means *any* regular, repeating pattern over a fixed timeframe. For example, weekly traffic peaks on weekdays, or hourly electricity spikes during the evening, are both seasonal cycles!

cards.title: ARIMA vs. SARIMA
cards.body: How standard ARIMA and Seasonal ARIMA handle cycles:

left.text:
Only models non-seasonal patterns.
Looks back at consecutive past steps (e.g. yesterday, day before).
Fails to capture repeating seasonal cycles.

right.text:
Models seasonal patterns too.
Looks at consecutive steps AND seasonal steps (e.g. last week, last year).
Perfect for seasonal repeating data.

exercises.prompt: What does the 'S' in SARIMA stand for?

options.text: Seasonal — because it models repeating cycles in data
Supervised — because it requires human supervision to train
Stochastic — because it deals with random variables

exercises.explain: Correct! The 'S' stands for Seasonal, allowing the model to capture repeating patterns over fixed time periods.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In time series modeling, 'seasonality' only refers to the four weather seasons.
exercises.explain: False. Seasonality refers to any pattern that repeats at regular intervals, such as weekly patterns (7 days) or hourly patterns (24 hours).

exercises.sentence: To forecast airline passenger numbers that peak every summer, we should use ___ instead of standard ARIMA.
exercises.explain: SARIMA is designed to handle seasonal fluctuations (like summer travel rushes) by modeling the cycles.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following time series shows strong weekly seasonality?

options.text: A gym's daily attendance peaking every Monday and Tuesday, then dropping every weekend
A company's stock price rising steadily over 5 years without repeating patterns
A coin flip outcome sequence (Heads, Tails, Heads, Heads)

exercises.explain: Yes! A pattern repeating every 7 days (peaking on Mondays/Tuesdays, dropping on weekends) represents weekly seasonality.

exercises.prompt: You are designing a forecasting system for a ski resort. The resort has a steady annual growth trend, day-to-day weather fluctuations, and a massive visitor spike every winter. Why is SARIMA the ideal choice here?

options.text: It combines ARIMA's ability to handle trends (I) and short-term shocks (MA) with seasonal components (S) to capture the annual winter spikes.
It uses standard linear regression to fit the sales of umbrellas.
It ignores the winter spikes and focuses only on the summer hiking season.
It is the only model that does not require any historical data to make a prediction.

exercises.explain: Phenomenal! SARIMA handles the trend, short-term fluctuations, and the annual seasonal pattern all in one elegant model. You've mastered the fundamentals of ARIMA and SARIMA! Next: how to evaluate if our forecasts are actually correct!
